chunks.text: Christos Papadimitriou is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley's EECS department. He earned his B.S. in EE from Athens Polytechnic in 1972, followed by a M.S. in EE and Ph.D. in EECS from Princeton in 1974 and 1976 respectively. He holds the C. Lester Hogan Professor of EECS title and has taught at Harvard, MIT, Athens Polytechnic, Stanford, and UCSD before joining UC Berkeley in January 1996.

He has authored several influential books, including 'Elements of the Theory of Computation' (with Harry Lewis, 1982/1997), 'Combinatorial Optimization: Algorithms and Complexity' (with Ken Steiglitz, 1982/1998), 'The Theory of Database Concurrency Control' (1988), 'Computational Complexity' (1994), and 'The Undergraduate Textbook Algorithms' (with Sanjoy Dasgupta and Umesh Vazirani, 2006). He also wrote a novel about computation titled 'Turing' (MIT Press 2003).

His research areas include Biosystems & Computational Biology (BIO) and Theory (THY). He is affiliated with several research centers: Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR), Berkeley Center for New Media (BCNM), CITRIS People and Robots (CPAR), and Industrial Engineering and Operations Research (IEOR).

Selected publications include works on BGP-based routing (2005), geographic routing (2003), cost-sharing in multicast (2001), latent semantic indexing analysis (2000), worst-case equilibria (1999), and early papers on parallel algorithms (1988) and database serializability (1979). Many are co-authored with notable researchers like J. Feigenbaum, S. Shenker, and M. Yannakakis.

He has received numerous honors and awards, including the IEEE CS Women of ENIAC Computer Pioneer Award (2022), IEEE John von Neumann Medal (2016), Gold-Platinum ETH Medal (2016), EATCS Award (2015), Gödel Prize (2012), Diane S. McEntyre Award for Teaching (2009), membership in NAS (2009) and NAE (2002), Katayanagi Prize (2008), Knuth Prize (2002), ACM Fellow (2001), and multiple ACM SIGMOD Best Paper Awards (1997, 2000).
